---
title: "U.S. Macro, Trade, and Commodity Dashboard"
subtitle: "Reproducible R workflow over FRED data — 13 series, quarterly panel with deflators and terms of trade, 8-quarter ARIMA forecasts, and an exchange-rate pass-through regression on U.S. trade prices."
date: last-modified
author: "Yullie Yang"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
library(readr)
library(knitr)
library(dplyr)

read_out <- function(p) suppressMessages(read_csv(p, show_col_types = FALSE))

descr        <- read_out("outputs/tables/descriptive_summary.csv")
model_sum    <- read_out("outputs/tables/model_summary.csv")
forecasts    <- read_out("outputs/tables/forecast_results.csv")
passthrough  <- read_out("outputs/tables/passthrough_coefficients.csv")
```

::: {.callout-note}
This dashboard is auto-rendered from the repository on every push to `main`
via a GitHub Actions workflow. All figures and tables shown below are the
exact artifacts checked into the repo under `outputs/`.
:::

## Overview

Thirteen FRED series — WTI crude oil, natural gas, copper, nominal and real
exports/imports, real GDP, unemployment, the effective federal funds rate,
CPI, industrial production, and the broad trade-weighted U.S. dollar index —
are pulled, harmonized to a common quarterly grid (period-average aggregation),
and combined into a wide analysis panel with derived measures:
net exports (nominal and real), trade balance ratio, **implicit import and
export deflators**, **terms of trade**, YoY / QoQ growth on every level
series, and log / quarterly-change of WTI.

The same pipeline then:

1. fits `forecast::auto.arima()` to three structurally different target series
   — **net exports**, **real GDP**, and **WTI crude oil** — and produces an
   8-quarter forecast with 80% / 95% prediction intervals for each, and
2. estimates a **distributed-lag exchange-rate pass-through** regression of
   Δlog(import / export deflator) on Δlog(broad USD) with 0–4 quarter lags
   and a CPI control, reporting the cumulative pass-through coefficient.

## Indicator overview

![](outputs/figures/01_macro_trade_overview.png){fig-alt="Multi-panel time series of macro, trade, and commodity indicators"}

## Net exports

![](outputs/figures/02_net_exports_trend.png){fig-alt="U.S. net exports of goods and services, quarterly, levels"}

## Co-movement

![](outputs/figures/03_correlation_heatmap.png){fig-alt="Pearson correlation heatmap across oil, trade, and macro variables"}

## Exchange-rate pass-through to U.S. import prices

![](outputs/figures/06_fx_vs_import_deflator.png){fig-alt="Contemporaneous co-movement scatter: Δlog(broad USD) vs Δlog(import deflator)"}

Cumulative 5-quarter pass-through coefficients (Σ β₀..β₄) from the
distributed-lag regression:

```{r passthrough-headline}
passthrough |>
  select(target, cumulative_passthrough, n_obs) |>
  distinct() |>
  mutate(cumulative_passthrough = round(cumulative_passthrough, 3)) |>
  kable(col.names = c("Trade-price series", "Cumulative pass-through (Σ β)", "n (quarters)"))
```

A negative coefficient indicates a dollar appreciation passes through to
lower U.S. import / export prices, as expected. Magnitudes well below one
in absolute value reflect the well-documented incomplete pass-through
characteristic of U.S. trade prices.

## Forecasts (8 quarters, 80% / 95% prediction intervals)

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Net exports

![](outputs/figures/04_forecast_net_exports.png)

Selected model: **ARIMA(0,1,0)** — a random-walk baseline. The flat point
forecast and fanning intervals are an honest acknowledgment that
quarterly U.S. net exports are close to a random walk and a univariate
ARIMA has little signal to extract.

### Real GDP

![](outputs/figures/05_forecast_real_gdp.png)

Selected model: **ARIMA(0,1,1) with drift**. The positive drift term
captures trend growth; the MA(1) on first differences absorbs
short-horizon noise.

### WTI crude oil

![](outputs/figures/07_forecast_oil_wti.png)

Selected model: **ARIMA(1,1,2)**. AR(1) coefficient ≈ 0.66 implies
moderate persistence of quarterly shocks; the two MA terms absorb noise.
The fan widens quickly — reflecting the well-known difficulty of
multi-quarter commodity-price forecasting.

:::

## Forecast results

```{r forecast-table}
forecasts |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 2))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Point forecast and 80% / 95% prediction intervals, 8-quarter horizon, per target.",
    col.names = c("Target", "Date", "Point", "Lower 80", "Upper 80", "Lower 95", "Upper 95")
  )
```

## Model summary

```{r model-table}
model_sum |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "ARIMA coefficient estimates and fit diagnostics for each target.",
    col.names = c("Target", "Model", "Term", "Estimate", "Std. error", "z", "p-value")
  )
```

## Pass-through regression — full coefficients

```{r passthrough-table}
passthrough |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Distributed-lag regression of Δlog(trade deflator) on Δlog(broad USD) with 0..4 quarter lags and Δlog(CPI) as a control. `is_fx` flags the dollar-lag terms whose coefficients sum to the cumulative pass-through."
  )
```

## Descriptive summary

```{r descr-table}
descr |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Per-series summary statistics on the cleaned quarterly panel."
  )
```

## Methodology highlights

- **Frequency alignment.** Daily and monthly series are aggregated to
  quarterly period averages; quarterly series pass through with their
  date normalized to quarter-start.
- **Derived measures.** Nominal and real net exports, trade balance ratio,
  implicit import / export deflators, terms of trade, YoY / QoQ growth on
  every level series, and log / quarterly-change transforms for WTI.
- **Forecast configuration.** `auto.arima()` with `seasonal = TRUE`,
  `stepwise = FALSE`, `approximation = FALSE` — i.e. the full model-space
  search rather than the fast heuristic. Horizon = 8 quarters, intervals
  at 80% and 95%.
- **Pass-through specification.** OLS distributed-lag regression with
  0–4 quarter lags of Δlog(broad USD) and a contemporaneous Δlog(CPI)
  control; cumulative pass-through is the sum of the five FX coefficients.
- **Reproducibility.** All paths via `here::here()`; FRED API key via an
  environment variable (`.Renviron.example` template, `.Renviron`
  git-ignored); a single command (`source(here::here("scripts",
  "05_generate_outputs.R"))`) rebuilds every artifact in `data/processed/`
  and `outputs/`.

See [`docs/methodology.md`](https://github.com/yullieyang/r-macro-trade-commodity-forecast/blob/main/docs/methodology.md),
[`docs/data_dictionary.md`](https://github.com/yullieyang/r-macro-trade-commodity-forecast/blob/main/docs/data_dictionary.md),
and [`docs/research_themes.md`](https://github.com/yullieyang/r-macro-trade-commodity-forecast/blob/main/docs/research_themes.md)
for the full versions.

## Limitations

This is a **baseline workflow**, not a production forecasting system.
Specifically:

- Univariate ARIMA only on the forecast side — no exogenous regressors,
  no multivariate structure (VAR / BVAR / factor models), no judgmental
  adjustment.
- Pass-through is single-equation OLS; a richer treatment would use IV,
  state-space pass-through with time-varying coefficients, or a structural
  model with strategic complementarities in price-setting.
- Quarterly aggregation discards intra-quarter dynamics in oil and FX.
- Unconditional forecasts; no scenario conditioning or structural-break
  handling (e.g. COVID).
- No automated refresh; reruns are manual.

---

*Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis (FRED). Pipeline source:
[`yullieyang/r-macro-trade-commodity-forecast`](https://github.com/yullieyang/r-macro-trade-commodity-forecast).*
